steps.title: Apply the quotient rule for exponents
steps.content:
When dividing powers with the same base, subtract the exponents. Apply this separately to $h$ and $q$:
$$\frac{h^{15}}{h^5} = h^{15-5} = h^{10}$$
$$\frac{q^{7}}{q^{21}} = q^{7-21} = q^{-14}$$

steps.title: Rewrite with positive exponents
steps.content:
A negative exponent moves the factor to the denominator:
$$q^{-14} = \frac{1}{q^{14}}$$
So the expression simplifies to $\dfrac{h^{10}}{q^{14}}$.

steps.title: Evaluate each choice
steps.content:
• Choice A — '$\frac{h^{10}}{q^{14}}$': $h^{15-5} = h^{10}$ in the numerator; $q^{7-21} = q^{-14} = \frac{1}{q^{14}}$ in the denominator. Correct. ✓
  • Choice B — '$\frac{h^{3}}{q^{3}}$': $h^{15} / h^5 = h^{10}$, not $h^3$; and $q^7 / q^{21} = q^{-14}$, not $q^{-3}$. Both exponents are wrong. ✗
  • Choice C — '$h^{10}q^{14}$': Gets the $h$ exponent right but misplaces $q$ in the numerator instead of the denominator. ✗
  • Choice D — '$h^{3}q^{3}$': Both exponents are wrong and $q$ is in the wrong position. ✗
That matches choice A — A is correct.
